coredata.dc:title: A novel probe for tetracyclines detection and its applications in cell imaging based on fluorescent WS2 quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Ge, Jia
Ma, Demiao
Duan, Guohang
Yan, Zhongyue
Yang, Like
Yang, Dan
Cai, Ren
Tan, Weihong
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a novel fluorescence sensor for tetracyclines (TCs) detection was designed using WS2 quantum dots (WS2 QDs). WS2 QDs could be quenched by TCs through the inner filter effect (IFE). The limit of detection of this proprosed method is 39 nM, 52 nM, and 28 nM for tetracycline (TC), doxycycline (DC), and oxytetracycline (OTC), respectively. The as-proposed strategy was successfully applied to detect TC in milk samples and human serum samples. The WS2 QDs were highly biocompatible and showed lower toxicity. Moreover, the WS2 QDs was successfully applied to imaging TC in HeLa cells owing to its excellent optical performance and great biocompatibility.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tetracyclines
Inner filter effect
WS2 quantum dots
Sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM imaging of WS2 QDs. (B) The size distribution for the WS2 QDs. (C–D) High resolution XPS spectrum of the W 4f peak and S 1s peak of the WS2 QDs.

(A) UV–vis absorption of tetracycline (curve a); UV–vis absorption of oxytetracycline (curve b); UV–vis absorption of doxycycline (curve c); fluorescence excitation band (curve d) and fluorescence emission band (curve e) of the WS2 QDs. (B) Fluorescence spectra of the WS2 QDs at different excitation wavelengths.

(A) The fluorescence emission spectra of WS2 QDs (curve a) and WS2 QDs + TC (curve b) under 375 nm excitation. (B) Fluorescence lifetime experiments of WS2 QDs without (red line) and with TC (blue line). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the WS2 QDs recorded with increasing TC concentration (from top to bottom, 0, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10, and 20 μM) under 375 nm excitation. (B) The relation between fluorescence intensity and amounts of TC.

FL intensity response of the WS2 QDs-based fluorescence sensor to different interfering substances: (A) some metal ions (K+, Zn2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Ca2+), amino acids (phenylalanine, arginine, glycine, leucine, lysine, valine, histidine, serine, threonine, cysteine), GSH, BSA, glucose; (B) various of antibiotics (chloramphenicol, streptomycin, cefotaxime, azithromycin, cephalosporin, gentamycin, ampicillin, kanamycin, tetracycline, doxycycline, and oxytetracycline).
Cell viability of Hela cells after incubation with different concentration of WS2 QDs by using MTT assay.
Fluorescence images of Hela cells incubated with (A) WS2 QDs and (B) WS2 QDs + TC.
Schematic diagram of the WS2 QDs as a nanosensor for TCs detection based on the IFE.
Comparison of analytical characteristics of various approaches for TCs detection.

row.entry: Fluorescence B, N codoped carbon dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of TC in milk samples and human serum samples by WS2 quantum dots (a The mean of three determinations.b SD = standard deviation.).

head.ce:title: A novel probe for tetracyclines detection and its applications in cell imaging based on fluorescent WS2 quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Molecular Science and Biomedicine Laboratory, State Key Laboratory for Chemo/Bio-Sensing and Chemometrics, College of Biology, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, and Collaborative Research Center of Molecular Engineering for Theranostics, Hunan University, Changsha, 410082, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a novel fluorescence sensor for tetracyclines (TCs) detection was designed using WS2 quantum dots (WS2 QDs). WS2 QDs could be quenched by TCs through the inner filter effect (IFE). The limit of detection of this proprosed method is 39 nM, 52 nM, and 28 nM for tetracycline (TC), doxycycline (DC), and oxytetracycline (OTC), respectively. The as-proposed strategy was successfully applied to detect TC in milk samples and human serum samples. The WS2 QDs were highly biocompatible and showed lower toxicity. Moreover, the WS2 QDs was successfully applied to imaging TC in HeLa cells owing to its excellent optical performance and great biocompatibility.

A simple and sensitive fluorescent sensor for tetracyclines assay was developed based on WS2 quantum dots.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracyclines (TCs), such as tetracycline (TC), doxycycline (DC) and oxytetracycline (OTC), have been widely used antibiotic for the treatment of common infections caused by clinical pathogens, such as mycoplasma pneumonia and urogenital tract infections [1–3]. However, the over-use of TCs results in high concentrations of drug residues in the body, which leads to adverse effects, including liver damage, bacterial resistances, allergic reactions, tooth discoloration, and gastrointestinal disorders [4–7]. Among numerous analytical methods, such as capillary electrophoresis, high performance liquid chromatography, liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry, and electrochemical methods, have been employed for TCs detection [8–13]. However, most of them are often time-consuming and require professional technical personnel and complex operations. Design of a facile and low-cost TCs analysis with both high sensitivity and selectivity is still in demand.

In molecular fluorescence work, the inner filter effect (IFE) refers to the absorption of the exciting and/or emitted light by absorbers in the sample itself [14]. In contrast to conventional detection systems, IFE-based strategy do not require surface modifications of fluorescent materials or covalent linking between a receptor and a fluorophore. This feature has led to the design of several novel fluorescence assays [15–18]. For example, as a fluorescence nanoprobe, carbon dots have been exploited for the detection of acetone via IFE [15]. A novel and sensitive sensor was described for organophosphorus pesticides assay with the IFE between Au nanoparticles and quantum dots [16]. Recently, tungsten disulfide (WS2) nanosheets have attracted extensive research interest due to the advantages of semiconducting electronic properties and relatively large surface areas [19–22]. In particular, when the widths of WS2 crystals were reduced to 5 nm or below, the as-obtained WS2 quantum dots (WS2 QDs) could generate strong photoluminescence because of their strong quantum confinement and edge effects [23]. Thus, due to their excellent luminescent capability, WS2 QDs have been applied in optical imaging and fluorescence sensing. For instance, WS2 QDs were employed as multifunctional nanotheranostics for in vivo dual-modal imaging [24]. Fluorescent WS2 QD probes were designed for nitrofurazone and lipoic acid assay [25,26]. However, the applications of WS2 QDs in fluorescence sensing have been limited, primarily because of their limited solubility in both aqueous and non-aqueous solvents. Thus, it is highly necessary to design an efficient method to fabricate water-soluble WS2 QDs and exploit their further applications in field of sensing and analysis.

Herein, we reported functional WS2 QDs as a fluorophore for an IFE-based sensor for TCs assay. WS2 QDs are synthesized by using sodium tungstate and cysteine via a hydrothermal synthesis (Scheme 1 ). Owing to the inner filter effect, WS2 QDs could be quenched by TCs (e.g., TC, DC, and OTC) as the absorber (Scheme 1). The limit of detection of this proprosed method is 39 nM, 52 nM, and 28 nM for TC, DC, and OTC, respectively. When it is applied to detect TCs in milk samples and human serum samples, this probe shows high sensitivity and recoveries in the range from 98.0% to 104.0% and 96.4% to 105%, respectively. Moreover, WS2 QDs could be used for the cell imaging with good biocompatibility.

The synthesis procedure of WS2 QDs was modified with sodium tungstate and cysteine as precursor via a simple hydrothermal process [27]. First, 0.025 g of sodium tungstate and 0.35 g of cysteine were dissolved in 4 mL water with stirring for 10 min until the solution cleared. The above mixture was heated at 220 °C for 10 h, and then cooled to 25 °C. The obtained WS2 QDs were collected by centrifugation at 12,000 rpm for 15 min and stored at 4 °C for further use.

The typical procedure for TCs analysis was realized by the following steps. First, 10 μL of WS2 QDs (0.6 mg/mL) solution, 20 μL of TCs solution (TC, DC, OTC) with different concentrations (0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10, and 20 μM) were added into 150 μL of PBS buffer (10 mM, pH = 7.4). The mixture was allowed to keep for 10 min at 25 °C. Finally, the fluorescent spectrum of the sensing system was collected with excitation wavelength of 375 nm.

The morphology of the WS2 QDs was examined using TEM, the as-obtained WS2 QDs were uniformly dispersed (Fig. 1 A). The WS2 QDs possessed a narrow size distribution with the average size of 2.85 ± 0.7 nm, which was determined from at least 100 particles in TEM image (Fig. 1B). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern displayed three well-defined peaks at 18.6°, 29.8°, and 49.2°, corresponding to the (002), (100), and (105) lattice planes of WS2 (Fig. S1), respectively [28]. What's more, XPS spectra was acquired to further analyze the elemental composition of the WS2 QDs. The peaks at 34.8 eV and 36.9 eV are assigned to the W 4f7/2 and W 4f5/2 levels in WS2 QDs, respectively (Fig. 1C), in good agreement with the W4+ oxidation state of 2-H WS2. The peaks at 167.5 eV and 170.4 eV corresponds to S 2p3/2 and 2p1/2, which confirmed the S (II) oxidation state (Fig. 1D).

The optical properties of WS2 QDs were also studied. On excitation at 375 nm, the maximum emission intensity was observed near 448 nm for the WS2 QDs (Fig. 2 A). The quantum yield of WS2 QDs was measured to be 9.6%, using quinine sulfate as the reference. As shown in Fig. 2B, the emission of WS2 QDs varied from 426 nm to 452 nm when the excitation wavelength increased from 350 nm to 430 nm. Therefore, WS2 QDs exhibited an excitation wavelength-independent feature, which could be tributed to the crystal structure or the surface state of WS2 QDs [29]. The effect of pH on the performance of WS2 QDs was studied. The FL intensity of WS2 QDs remained almost constant in the pH range of 4.5–8.0 (Fig. S2). Furthermore, the FL intensity of WS2 QDs was unchanged in the range of 0 mM–500 mM NaCl solution (Fig. S3). Therefore, these WS2 QDs showed a high stability against the change of ionic strength in the aqueous solution, which is important for their sensing applications in high-salt environments. Meanwhile, irradiation of the WS2 QDs solution for 1 h decreased the fluorescence intensity of the solution by 3%, which was indicative of their good photostability (Fig. S4). All these features indicate that the as-synthesized WS2 QDs are suitable for sensing or other biological applications.

As shown in Fig. 3 A, WS2 QDs exhibited strong fluorescence signal with excitation at 375 nm (curve a). The FL intensity of the WS2 QDs was significantly quenched in the presence of TC (curve b). As shown in Fig. 2A, there is a large overlap between the TCs absorption spectrum and the fluorophore excitation spectrum of WS2 QDs, indicative of the IFE mechanism. Fig. S5 showed the effect of different incubation times after the addition of TC. The fluorescence intensity of the sensing system decreased rapidly with the incubation time from 0 to 5 min, the fluorescence response reached the equilibrium, then, 10 min is selected as the optimal detection time. The response affected by reaction time with the addition of DC and OTC is shown in Fig. S6. When the reaction time was 10 min after the addition of DC and OTC, the fluorescence response reached the equilibrium, respectively. So, 10 min was selected as the optimal reaction time as the same as TC. Furthermore, we explored the TCs-induced fluorescence quenching mechanism. The excitation spectrum of WS2 QDs and the absorption spectrum of TC exhibited a significant overlap, suggesting that fluorescence of WS2 QDs can be quenched by TC via IFE or FRET [30]. Since the quenching mechanism of FRET and IFE can be readily distinguished by observing the change of fluorescence lifetime. Fluorescence lifetime could be shortened in an efficient energy transfer process, but not for IFE [31]. The fluorescence decay curves of WS2 QDs in the absence and presence of TC exhibited a high degree of consistency (Fig. 3B). The average fluorescence lifetime of WS2 QDs (4.76 ns) remains unchanged with/without TC. Furthermore, the fluorescence lifetimes of the WS2 QDs without and with DC are measured to be 4.73 ns and 5.15 ns, respectively (Fig. S7A). The fluorescence lifetimes of the WS2 QDs without and with OTC are measured to be 4.62 ns and 4.88 ns, respectively (Fig. S7B). These results further demonstrated the occurrence of IFE between WS2 QDs and TCs.

The sensitivity of the proposed strategy was studied under optimal conditions. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence intensity of WS2 QDs dramatically decreased when the TC concentration increased from 0 μM to 20 μM. The linear response range of the as-proposed fluoresence platform for TC detection with the range of 0.1–10 μM by the linear equation of F = −83.45C tetracycline + 1198.7 (R2 = 0.9916) and the limit of detection (LOD) of 39 nM (S/N = 3, n = 11) (Fig. 4B). Fig.S8B shows that a linear relationship of F versus DC concentration with the regression equation: F = −79.57 Cdoxycycline + 1216.4 (R2 = 0.9964) with the LOD is 52 nM (S/N = 3, n = 11). Fig.S9B shows that a linear relationship of F versus OTC concentration with the regression equation: F = −85.69 Coxytetracycline + 1174.3 (R2 = 0.9932) with the LOD is 28 nM (S/N = 3, n = 11). As listed in Table 1 , the linear range, calibration sensitivity, and LOD of this proposed method were comparable and even superior to than other nanostructures for TCs detection, such as Ru(bpy)32+-doped silica nanoparticles (230 nM for TC) [32], Cs4PbBr6/CsPbBr3 perovskite nanoparticles (76 nM for TC) [33], boron nitride quantum dot (19 nM for TC and 104 nM for OTC) [34], gold nanoclusters (563 nM for TC and 664 nM for OTC) [35], terbium metal-organic framework (184 nM for TC) [36], B, N codoped carbon dots (170 nM for TC) [37], Ti3C2 MXene QDs (20 nM for TC) [38], Al-MOF (26.16 nM for TC, 40.36 nM for DC, and 62.05 nM for OTC) [39], carbon dots@hierarchical mesoporous ZIF-8 (6.56 nM for TC, 30.58 nM for DC, and 29.46 nM for OTC) [40], carbon dots (160 nM for TC and 230 nM for OTC) [41], S,N-doped carbon dots (250 nM for TC, 390 nM for DC, and 260 nM for OTC) [42]. These results demonstrated the good sensitivity of our WS2 QDs for TCs detection.

To further study the selectivity for TCs detection in real samples, the responses of coexisting potentially interfering antibiotics and analytes, such as other antibiotics (i.e., chloramphenicol, streptomycin, cefotaxime, azithromycin, cephalosporin, gentamycin, ampicillin, and kanamycin), biological ions (i.e., K+, Zn2+, Co2+, Mg2+, and Ca2+), amino acids (i.e., phenylalanine, serine, valine, arginine, glycine, leucine, lysine, histidine, threonine, and cysteine), BSA, glucose, and GSH were investigated. As shown in Fig. 5 , only TC antibiotics (TC, DC, and OTC) could quench the fluorescence intensity of WS2 QDs, and the fluorescence intensity of the system had no obvious changed after addition of other interfering antibiotics and analytes, this IFE-based sensor is considered to be selective for TCs.

To evaluate the applicability of the method in real samples, such as milk powder and human serum, TCs as mode were examined by the standard addition method. For milk powder samples, a good linearity was obtained in the concentration ranged from 0.1 to 10 μM with R2 = 0.9949 (Fig. S10A). Briefly, various concentrations of TC (0.5 μM, 1 μM, 2 μM) were added to the designated milk powders. The recovery of TC was in the range of 98.0%–104.0%, and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) were 2.2%–3.2% (Table 2 ). For human serum samples, a good linearity was also obtained in the concentration ranged from 0.1 to 10 μM with R2 = 0.9952 (Fig. S10B). The recovery experiments with different amount of TC (0.5 μM, 1 μM, 2 μM) were also performed in 1% diluted human serum. The RSDs were obtained by the proposed method, which were 2.4%–2.8% with the recoveries ranging from 96.4% to 103.5%. Meanwhile, all the results were consistent with those from HPLC (Table 2). The standard addition method was conducted on the milk samples and human serum samples by spiking with different concentrations of DC and OTC (0.5 μM, 1 μM, and 2 μM). For the milk samples, the recoveries are 96%–104.5% and 93%–96% for DC and OTC, respectively (Table S1). For human serum samples, the recoveries are 93.6%–97.5% and 94.4%–97% for DC and OTC, respectively (Table S2). Meanwhile, all tested results were consistent with those from HPLC. All results demonstrated the potential of our proposed method as a highly efficient nanosensor for the determination of TCs in real samples.

Moreover, we have evaluated the feasibility of WS2 QDs for its further biological applications, especially in the field of cellular imaging. The cytotoxic effect of WS2 QDs was investigated first before imaging studies by using MTT assay. Hela cells were incubated with different amount of WS2 QDs (25, 50, 100, 200, 300, and 400 μg/mL) for 24 h. As indicated in Fig. 6 , almost 86% of Hela cells remained alive when the level of WS2 QDs was as high as 400 μg/mL. This result reveals the good biocompatibility of WS2 QDs. Hence, WS2 QDs could hold great promise as a good cellular imaging nanoprobe for monitoring TC assay because of their low cytotoxicity. Herein, the characteristic optical, chemical and physical properties, such as low toxicity, excellent aqueous stability and resistance to photobleaching of WS2 QDs, make WS2 QDs be worked as a imaging agent. As shown in Fig. 7 A, the Hela cells treated with WS2 QDs showed noticeable blue fluorescence from the intracellular region, indicating the occurrence of cellular uptake of WS2 QDs. However, when 100 μM TC was added into the above cells loaded with WS2 QDs for 30 min, the intracellular fluorescence of Hela cells became very weak, which indicated WS2 QDs, as imaging agent, could be used for monitoring the TC in living cells (Fig. 7B). All results indicated that indicating that WS2 QDs are remarkable materials for cellular imaging applications.

In summary, a facile and low-cost method for TCs detection with both high sensitivity and selectivity was developed based on the IFE of TCs on the fluorescence of WS2 QDs. The limit of detection of this method is 39 nM, 52 nM, and 28 nM for tetracycline, doxycycline, and oxytetracycline, respectively. The sensor demonstrates good recovery for the analysis of TCs in milk samples and human serum samples. Moreover, the use of biomass materials as a precursor without any surface modification is environment friendly, and the WS2 QDs are successfully employed as an imaging agent for TC cell imaging due to their good biocompatibility. The as-proposed strategy does not require sophisticated instrumentation or any modification or covalent linking between a receptor and fluorophore. The procedure is convenient and accomplished in homogeneous solution, and response is achieved rapidly after simple mixing. We believe that this WS2 QD-based sensing strategy would serve as a simple and efficient platform for fluorescent determination mode in analytical areas.

Jia Ge: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – original draft. Demiao Ma: Data curation, Investigation. Guohang Duan: Data curation, Investigation. Zhongyue Yan: Data curation, Investigation. Like Yang: Data curation, Investigation. Dan Yang: Conceptualization, Methodology. Ren Cai: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Weihong Tan: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22004032), the Natural Science Foundation of Henan Province (212300410408), China, Hunan Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (2021JJ20020), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (531118010569), China.
